title: Will a Functioning Government Come Soon?
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The Tamil below is a source-checked reading copy: the opening salutation omitted by OCR has been restored from the printed page, and clear OCR numeral, word, spacing, and line-join errors have been repaired. The canonical source file remains unchanged. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement.
paragraphs: Kerala Irrigation Minister P. J. Joseph has said that his Government has decided to petition the Supreme Court again, seeking to reduce the Mullaperiyar dam's water level to 136 feet. The Supreme Court has permitted Tamil Nadu to raise it to 142 feet, giving the State a valuable opportunity. Heavy rain in Kerala has rapidly raised the level, which reached 138 feet on 5 November 2014, for the first time in about thirty-five years.

Kerala officials told the Supreme Court-appointed committee that the rising water had frightened people near the dam and sought a reduction to 136 feet in the interest of their safety. The committee rejected that request.

An emergency meeting of irrigation officials was therefore held in Thiruvananthapuram on 5 November under Minister Joseph. Afterwards he said the level had crossed 138.10 feet, causing panic, and that Kerala would approach the Supreme Court on the seventh seeking a reduction to 136 feet and request a hearing by the eleventh.

On reading this, I telephoned our Cumbum MLA, brother N. Ramakrishnan, who has shown sustained concern for Mullaperiyar, and asked him for the current facts. He supplied several details, and this letter follows from them.

When separate cases concerning the dam were pending in the Kerala and Tamil Nadu High Courts, the DMK Government petitioned the Supreme Court on 14 February 1998 to have them heard together. On 1 February 1999, the Supreme Court stayed all related proceedings in the High Courts and transferred the case to itself.

The Court then urged the Union Government to explore a settlement. The Union Water Resources Minister asked the Chief Ministers of Tamil Nadu and Kerala to hold direct talks. Meetings in Thiruvananthapuram on 5 April 2000 and New Delhi on 19 May produced no result because of Kerala's obstinacy. The Supreme Court then ordered an expert committee.

The Union Water Resources Ministry constituted a committee headed by Dr Mittal, with K. S. Kanpur, Dr R. S. Varshney, J. K. Tiwari, A. K. Kanju, A. Mohanakrishnan and M. K. Parameswaran Nair. It extensively inspected Mullaperiyar on 10 and 11 October 2000. A committee headed by Brar then inspected the Baby Dam between 20 and 25 November. Their final report, submitted on 30 August 2001, recommended storing water up to 142 feet until strengthening of the Baby Dam was complete, and up to 152 feet afterwards.

The historic judgment in the case pursued under the DMK Government was delivered on 27 February 2006 by a Supreme Court bench headed by Chief Justice Sabharwal. It held the dam to be strong, allowed storage up to 142 feet, and said that after the remaining strengthening work another committee could consider raising the level to its full height of 152 feet.

But the AIADMK Government then in office did not immediately implement the judgment and lower the shutters to secure Tamil Nadu's right to 142 feet. When questioned in the Assembly, then Chief Minister Jayalalithaa said there was insufficient water, summer had arrived, and inflow had fallen for want of rain.

Kerala used that opportunity to enact emergency legislation barring storage above 136 feet. Its Assembly also declared Kerala's authority to maintain all dams in the State, including Mullaperiyar, and build a new dam if necessary.

Because the Jayalalithaa Government failed to implement the judgment immediately, Tamil Nadu lost that opportunity to exercise its right to 142 feet. After further investigations under the Supreme Court's direct supervision, a final judgment again permitting storage up to 142 feet came on 7 May 2014.
Now heavy rain in the catchment has steadily raised the level. The moment has arrived to use the opportunity secured by that long legal struggle and store water up to 142 feet.

Yet until 5 November only 456 cubic feet per second was being released for drinking water, while on 6 November the release was abruptly increased to 1,816 cusecs. Why? When inflow is 1,973 cusecs, this increased discharge makes farmers and people of the five neighbouring districts wonder whether the Government is unwilling to implement the Supreme Court judgment.

Tamil farmers there say Kerala's fresh litigation is merely a drama intended to protect encroaching buildings erected in the Mullaperiyar catchment, and that there is no other mystery. The AIADMK Government has so far offered no explanation on this vital issue of irrigation and drinking water.

Tamil Nadu has not forgotten how the AIADMK hid the true history of the DMK Government's Supreme Court case and organised a celebration in Madurai to praise itself, without inviting those who raised their voices and fought for Mullaperiyar. People in the area now reportedly suspect that Jayalalithaa may fear that acting on the judgment and storing water to 142 feet would bring credit to the area's “proxy Chief Minister,” O. Panneerselvam.

At a time when continuous rain has filled every tank and saturated the land, unnecessary excess releases may cause Tamil Nadu to lose this chance. I insist that the Government immediately release only what is needed for drinking water, retain the inflow, uphold the final Supreme Court judgment in the case pursued under the DMK Government, and fulfil the farmers' long-standing aspiration.

At a felicitation in Madurai on 22 August, Jayalalithaa said steps would be taken to raise the level to 152 feet. What steps she has taken is known only to her. Now that Kerala has announced a Supreme Court petition seeking 136 feet, urgently defending the order permitting 142 feet is essential. Senior Tamil Nadu engineers expect the State to file a caveat. The Government must stop delaying, understand the facts, obtain advice from senior engineers such as Mohanakrishnan and Subramaniam, and for once act with some energy instead of behaving as though the matter does not concern it.

There is another issue. The Amaravathi dam near Udumalpet irrigates about one lakh acres in Tiruppur, Erode and Karur districts, benefiting more than fifty thousand farmers. Twenty-six combined drinking-water schemes on the Amaravathi serve more than two lakh people in over three hundred villages.

Its three principal sources are the Pambar from Kerala, the Chinnar through Valparai, and the Thenar from Kodaikanal, with the Pambar contributing the most. Kerala's Chief Minister has now inaugurated by video the Pattisseri dam project across the Pambar. If implemented, it will severely affect Tamil farmers.

The Cauvery agreement bars obstruction of water flowing into the Amaravathi, a Cauvery tributary, and bars construction of a dam across it. Related litigation between Tamil Nadu and Kerala is pending in the Supreme Court. Kerala is also said to have obtained no approval from the Union environment and power authorities, the Forest Department, or the Cauvery Tribunal. Tamil Nadu must therefore act immediately through the Union Government to stop this effort.

In a democracy, anyone may contest an election, come to power, and leave. But government is permanent. It must keep moving, not stagnate. If it thinks only of “Amma,” the wheels of administration will not turn.

The Tamil Nadu Government must begin functioning to protect the people's welfare. If the present condition continues, correcting all the resulting mistakes and damage will become an enormous ordeal. Let a Government for the people of this State begin to work; let it remove their hardships!
With affection,
M.K.
